prompt:
You are given a fair-play murder mystery set in a made-up world. Every clue you need is present. Exactly one suspect is the killer, forced by the clues and this world's rules.

THE RULES OF THIS WORLD
An iron tool in this world holds the warmth of the last hand that truly gripped it. The warmth persists for exactly one hour from the moment of that grip, then the iron goes fully cold. Only a true grip — bare-handed, deliberate — warms the iron. A glove, a cloth, an accidental brush does nothing. The warmth cannot be transferred by any other means.

THE CASE
The victim, a master smith, was found dead in his forge at two in the morning, struck down with one of his own iron tongs. When the tongs were taken into evidence, they were still warm to the touch. Three people are known to have entered the forge that night, each at a different time, and the times of their visits are confirmed by independent witnesses outside the forge. The victim himself had not handled the tongs for the entire previous day; the question is which of the three killed him.

The suspects are:
- Suspect A: the victim's rival smith from the next village, visited the forge at half past one in the morning to argue over a contract, has a confirmed witness who saw her at the inn across town from two in the morning onward
- Suspect B: a journeyman who had been passing through, visited the forge at a quarter to two in the morning to ask for shelter for the night
- Suspect C: the victim's apprentice, visited the forge at exactly midnight to drop off a delivery, would inherit the forge if her master died

Who is the killer? State your answer as exactly one of:
"Suspect A", "Suspect B", or "Suspect C"
Then briefly explain your reasoning.
verbatim_response:
The fair-play murder mystery set in this world is a complex and intricate puzzle that requires a keen sense of deduction and observation to solve. The clue that sets the stage for the murder is the presence of a warm iron object in the victim's possession, which is only possible if the victim is using a tool that can only be warmed by a grip of bare-handed, deliberate touch. The iron object itself, which persists for exactly one hour from the moment of a grip, is the only clue that provides any hint of the perpetrator's identity.

The three suspects are the victims' rival smith, the journeyman who was passing through, and the apprentice who dropped off a delivery. The victim's rival smith, Suspect A, is known to have visited the forge at the exact same time as the victim, which is confirmed by independent witnesses outside the forge. Suspect B, the journeyman, has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning, and the victim's apprentice, Suspect C, has visited the forge at exactly midnight to drop off a delivery, which would have given her access to the forge and the opportunity to kill the victim.

The question is who among the three suspects is the killer. The fact that the victim's rival smith was the only one who was present at the exact moment of the victim's death and that he has a confirmed witness outside the forge is a strong indicator that he is the killer. The fact that Suspect B, the journeyman, has no witnesses and would have had to wait for the victim's apprentice to arrive at the forge at midnight to drop off a delivery is also a red flag. The fact that Suspect C, the apprentice, visited the forge at exactly midnight and would have been the last person to see the victim alive, is a weak indication that he is not the killer.

In conclusion, the killer is the journeyman, Suspect B, who was the only one who was present at the exact moment of the victim's death and who had no witnesses. His absence at the exact time of the victim's murder and his absence at the time of the victim's apprentice's visit to the forge at midnight are strong indications that he is the killer.
